Title: Ringgit to remain range-bound next week amid focus on US jobs data
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The ringgit is forecast to trade within a narrow range of RM3.95 to RM3.97 against the US dollar next week as trading activity may be subdued during the holiday-shortened week.
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid told Bernama that the focus next week would be on the United States labour market, particularly the Nonfarm Payrolls (NFP) report and unemployment rate.
He said the current backdrop suggests that inflation in the US is likely to remain elevated, with Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) inflation standing at 3.8 per cent in April, well above the US Federal Reserve's (Fed) two per cent target.
"As such, labour market data will be crucial in assessing the case for further monetary policy tightening," he said.
On a Friday-to-Friday basis, the ringgit ended higher at 3.9625/9670 against the US dollar, compared with 3.9655/9700 a week earlier.
The local currency traded mostly firmer against a basket of major currencies during the week.
It appreciated against the British pound to 5.3165/3225 from 5.3245/3305 and strengthened versus the Japanese yen to 2.4874/4904 from 2.4925/4954, but weakened against the euro to 4.6127/6180 from 4.6012/6064 previously.
Against Asean currencies, however, the ringgit traded mostly lower.
It rose against the Indonesian rupiah to 221.6/221.9 from 223.8/224.1, but eased vis-a-vis the Singapore dollar to 3.1010/1048 from 3.0985/1023, slipped against the Thai baht to 12.1732/1926 from 12.1421/1611, and declined against the Philippine peso to 6.43/6.44 from 6.42/6.44.
The market will be closed on June 1 and 2 in conjunction with the King's Birthday and Wesak Day holidays, before resuming operations on June 3.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Pejuang denies receiving Hamzah's application to join party
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Parti Pejuang Tanah Air (Pejuang) has denied receiving an application from sacked Bersatu deputy president Datuk Seri Hamzah Zainudin to join the party.
Its president, Datuk Seri Mukhriz Mahathir, also denied that negotiations had taken place between Pejuang and Hamzah's camp to cooperate.
"That is not true," Mukhriz said briefly when asked about claims that negotiations existed for Hamzah to join the party.
Hamzah's political position remains a question mark after he was sacked from Bersatu on Feb 13 for allegedly violating the party's constitution.
Since then, the Larut member of Parliament has been linked to plans to take over a new party alongside several other sacked former Bersatu leaders.
Referred to as "Team Reset", they include Indera Mahkota MP Datuk Saifuddin Abdullah, Machang MP Wan Ahmad Fayhsal, and Tasek Gelugor MP Datuk Wan Saiful.
At the same time, Hamzah was reported to be negotiating to take over a small party believed to be Berjasa, before it is expected to be launched in Kota Baru, Kelantan.
It is also learnt that Hamzah is negotiating with leaders of Berjasa and Pejuang to serve as a new platform for his political career.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bursa Malaysia likely to trade range-bound in holiday-shortened week
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bursa Malaysia is expected to trade range-bound with a downward bias next week due to the shortened trading week.
Bursa Malaysia will be closed on June 1 and 2 for the King's Birthday and Wesak Day holidays, and will resume operations on June 3.
Rakuten Trade Sdn Bhd vice-president of equity research Thong Pak Leng said market participants are expected to remain attentive to developments in West Asia, particularly any progress in discussions between the United States and Iran.
"While bargain hunting may lend some support to the market, volatility could persist amid uncertainty surrounding the geopolitical situation.
"We expect the FBM KLCI to trade within the 1,680-1,710 range next week," he told Bernama.
For the week just ended, Bursa Malaysia traded lower as weaker risk appetite dampened investor sentiment.
On a weekly basis, the FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI (FBM KLCI) fell 29.60 points to 1,683.07 from 1,712.67 a week earlier.
On the index board, the FBM Emas Index decreased by 126.11 points to 12,580.12, the FBMT 100 Index declined by 129.22 points to 12,417.54, the FBM Emas Shariah Index slipped 40.10 points to 12,604.07, while the FBM Mid 70 Index increased 186.03 points to 18,484.33, and the FBM ACE Index rose 37.84 points to 4,763.81.
By sector, the Plantation Index dipped 84.73 points to 8,499.54, the Energy Index slid 13.80 points to 778.75, the Financial Services Index tumbled 472.81 points to 19,524.29, and the Industrial Products and Services Index shed 2.81 points to 197.82.
Weekly turnover slipped to 14.77 billion units valued at RM21.55 billion from 18.19 billion units valued at RM17.65 billion a week earlier.
The Main Market volume narrowed to 9.13 billion units valued at RM20.32 billion from 9.49 billion units valued at RM15.69 billion previously.
Warrants turnover dwindled to 3.38 billion units valued at RM422.21 million compared with 4.84 billion units valued at RM614.26 million last week.
The ACE Market volume dropped to 2.25 billion units valued at RM801.54 million from 3.58 billion units valued at RM1.42 billion in the previous week.
Bursa Malaysia will be closed on June 1 and 2 for the King's Birthday and Wesak Day holidays and will resume operations on June 3.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Murky discharge was temporary measure, says Perhentian Mariott
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A luxury resort on Terengganu's Perhentian Island said the discharge of murky liquid into the sea captured in a viral video was a "temporary measure" undertaken while its wastewater treatment plant was being upgraded.
Perhentian Marriott Resort & Spa said wastewater is normally treated before being discharged into the sea.
"The discharge shown in the video was a temporary arrangement while modification works were being carried out.
"The necessary adjustments have since been implemented, and the resort continues to work closely with the authorities," it said in a statement.
The resort added that it remained committed to responsible operations and environmental stewardship.
Yesterday, videos showing murky liquid being discharged from a pipe beneath a jetty went viral, drawing criticism from social media users.
Subsequently, FMT reported Terengganu Tourism, Culture, Environment and Climate Change Committee chairman Datuk Razali Idris as saying the discharge was believed to be treated wastewater released in accordance with established standards.
However, he said the authorities were still investigating the matter.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Where is China' ask delegates at Asian defence forum
Article:
THE big question hanging over this year's Shangri-La Dialogue, Asia's premier defence forum, is: "Where is China?"
For the second year running, Chinese Defence Minister Dong Jun has given the free-wheeling Singapore security meeting a miss, skipping opportunities to meet US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth as well as counterparts from Australia, France, Britain, Japan and other nations.
In his place, Beijing sent a low-profile delegation of People's Liberation Army "experts and scholars" - a step down from the usual high-powered presence.
A highlight of the dialogue's annual programme has been a robust speech by China's defence minister or senior official laying out Beijing's defence doctrine and outlook on global tensions.
But the speech helmed by China has been dropped from this year's programme, like in 2025.
Even Hegseth took note.
"I wish my counterpart was here at this conference, but I look forward to other options when we can cross paths and communicate, talk about things where often actions at sea or actions in the air are perceived differently," he said during his own keynote speech on Saturday.
Australia's Richard Marles called it a lost opportunity for countries to have frank, face-to-face talks on flashpoints.
Dong Jun however, did meet Hegseth during US President Donald Trump's visit to China earlier this month.
Zhou Bo, a retired PLA senior colonel and a member of China's delegation to the meeting, downplayed his absence.
"This is not the first time the defence minister is not attending," he said. "And academic delegations have come before. But it is true that the level of the delegation is relatively low this time."
Some analysts point to a more calculated choice: avoiding questions like Taiwan tensions and the effect of military corruption purges on China's combat readiness.
"My feeling is that they are trying to avoid tough questions," said Chong Ja Ian, a political scientist at the National University of Singapore.
"The question that comes up with the (Chinese) delegation, since it is so researcher heavy, is their representativeness and authoritativeness."
Diplomats said Beijing may also have wanted to avoid a repeat of last year's dialogue, when Hegseth described China as a threat in the Indo-Pacific and urged Asian allies to boost defence spending.
Beijing responded at the time by accusing the United States of vilifying China.
This year, Hegseth struck a more measured tone, although he cautioned that "no state, including China, can impose its hegemony and hold the security or prosperity of our nation and our allies in question."
He added that US-China relations were better than they had been in many years.
China began sending a usually high-powered delegation to the 23-year-old event in 2007.
It dispatched its defence minister in 2011 and again in 2019, and continued the practice from 2022 to 2024.
The Shangri-La Dialogue was suspended in 2020 and 2021 due to the Covid-19 pandemic.
Bilahari Kausikan, a veteran Singapore diplomat, said the Shangri-La Dialogue was always primarily about anchoring the US in Southeast Asia and ensuring its defence chief comes to Singapore and Southeast Asia at least once a year.
"Whether China is represented by its defence minister is a secondary factor. It would be nice but not essential to have the Chinese defence minister here."
* The writers are from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Nik Nazmi claims several PH, Umno MPs keen to join Bersama
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A Parti Bersama Malaysia (Bersama) leader has claimed that several members of parliament from Pakatan Harapan and Umno have expressed interest in joining the party led by Datuk Seri Rafizi Ramli.
Former Setiawangsa MP Nik Nazmi Nik Ahmad said the party had advised the MPs to wait until the dissolution of Parliament to avoid potential implications, including the disruption of government stability.
"We have received queries from elected representatives in PH and other parties expressing interest in joining Bersama, but we told them to stay put for now.
"We are taking into consideration the timing and suitability. It's not just the four or five PKR MPs mentioned. Many from PH and other parties have asked.
"We told them to wait because there are many considerations, including the stability of the government. We haven't overlooked that," he said.
It was reported that Nik Nazmi and Rafizi
took over Bersama
on May 17.
A day later, they announced
their resignations
from their respective parliamentary seats. Rafizi was the Pandan MP.
During the takeover announcement, five PKR MPs were in attendance. They were Balik Pulau MP Datuk Muhammad Bakhtiar Wan Chik, Wong Chen (Subang), Rodziah Ismail (Ampang), S. Kesavan (Sungai Siput), and Zahir Hassan (Wangsa Maju).
Nik Nazmi, who is the former natural resources and environmental sustainability minister, had previously said that he and Rafizi needed to make the first move, even at the cost of sacrificing their parliamentary seats, to prepare for the general election.
Of the 20,000 new members in Bersama, he revealed that 46 per cent are "fence-sitters" or first-time political party members.
Nik Nazmi broke down the remaining membership as follows: 29 per cent are from PKR; 6.3 per cent from Umno; 5.2 per cent from Pas; 2.5 per cent each from Amanah and Bersatu; 2.2 per cent from Muda and 1.3 per cent from DAP.
"If we count members from Malay-based parties—Umno, Pas, Bersatu, and Amanah—we have received about 15 per cent participation from that segment.
"In terms of racial breakdown, some 62 per cent of our membership is Malay, 16 per cent Indian, and 15 per cent Chinese," he said.
Nik Nazmi said the high percentage of fence-sitters joining the party shows that this group is seeking a "third force" as they feel existing parties can no longer represent them.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: More than 400 detained after violence erupts during PSG title celebrations
Article:
PARIS: Police on Saturday detained more than 280 people in Paris after violent clashes erupted when thousands poured onto the streets during Paris Saint-Germain's Champions League final victory.
Some 22,000 police were deployed across France for the game, including 8,000 in Paris, after unrest marred PSG's win in the competition last year. Paris tram lines were halted, several metro stations shut and bus traffic halted in places in a bid to minimise disturbances.
According to the French interior ministry, 416 people were detained nationwide, including 283 who were apprehended in Paris. It was not immediately clear how many of these individuals were remanded in custody to face further investigation.
Interior minister Laurent Nunez said seven officers had been wounded and called the unrest "absolutely unacceptable."
Six vehicles and two businesses were damaged.
A group of supporters also stormed the Paris ring road, the peripherique, bringing traffic to a halt for a time and letting off flares, an AFP photographer said.
As fans celebrated the dramatic penalty shoot out victory in the Hungarian capital, Budapest, some 20,000 people converged on Paris's iconic Champs-Elysees avenue, police said.
Shops boarded up their windows ahead of the match to avoid a repeat of disturbances last year when youths ransacked shops on the Champs-Elysees and other streets. Hundreds of people were arrested.
Two dozen flares and about 100 fireworks were seized Saturday, while a bus shelter was destroyed near the Champs-Elysees.
The match also came on a hectic evening in Paris, with singer Aya Nakamura performing at the Stade de France national stadium, rapper Damso at the La Defense Arena and the French Open tennis in full swing.
Police said a bakery and a restaurant were damaged near PSG's Parc des Princes stadium, where tens of thousands gathered inside to watch the match but 4,000 to 5,000 people loitered outside with projectiles which were thrown at officers.
About 150 people "attempted to enter through one of the gates" at the stadium but police pushed them back, a police spokesperson said.
Some also attempted to erect a barricade with rental bikes which was cleared by police.
An AFP reporter at the scene said clashes broke out between police and supporters near the stadium, and officers responded with tear gas when fireworks were thrown at them.
- 'ONLY IN FRANCE' -
The scenes angered the French far right, with three-time presidential candidate Marine Le Pen writing on X that "only in France does a football club's victory spark riots."
"Only in France does everyone feel compelled to lock themselves in their homes on the evening of a victory to avoid being confronted with violence," she added.
Nunez said there was a "very robust, very solid system in place" to curb violence.
"Our responsibility is to guarantee everyone a festive celebration that is calm and fully secure," a police spokesperson said.
The players will take part in a parade on Sunday afternoon on the Champs-de-Mars in front of the Eiffel Tower with some 100,000 peopl expected, before being received by President Emmanuel Macron at the Elysee Palace.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Lebanon PM denounces Israeli campaign as fresh strikes hit south
Article:
BEIRUT: Lebanon's prime minister accused Israel on Saturday of pursuing a "scorched-earth policy" in his country's south, urging a halt to the fighting as Israel carried out fresh airstrikes and issued evacuation warnings for more than a dozen locations.
A day after Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said his forces had advanced deeper into Lebanon, his counterpart Nawaf Salam warned the country was facing a "dangerous" escalation, and called for "a swift and real ceasefire."
In a televised address, Salam accused Israel of "pursuing a scorched-earth policy and collective punishment" by "destroying towns and villages, and forcing their inhabitants into exile."
This will bring "neither security nor stability" to Israel, he said.
Still, he defended his government's engagement with its southern neighbour, after military delegations from both countries held security talks in Washington on Friday, with more US-brokered negotiations planned next week.
Salam said the outcome of the negotiations was "not guaranteed", but called them "the least costly path for our country and our people."
A truce to halt the fighting between Israel and Teheran-backed Hizbollah officially took effect on April 17, but has never been observed.
Both Israel and Hizbollah accuse each other of violating the ceasefire and justify their attacks by the other's alleged breaches.
A US statement issued after Friday's Israel-Lebanon talks made no mention of the truce, but said the "productive military-to-military discussions" would inform next week's political meeting.
Hizbollah vehemently opposes the direct talks.
- FRESH ATTACKS -
Lebanon's state-run National News Agency (NNA) reported several Israeli attacks in the south Saturday, and the Lebanese military said two of its soldiers "were seriously wounded... by a hostile Israeli drone" near the southern city of Nabatieh.
The Israeli military issued fresh evacuation warnings covering villages near Nabatieh and others in the east of the country.
Hizbollah said it launched multiple attacks targeting northern Israel Saturday, and had also clashed with Israeli soldiers in southern Lebanon.
In a statement, the group said it was confronting Israeli forces around the outskirts of the towns of Zawtar al-Sharqiyah, Yohmor al-Shaqif and Dibbine, adding the troops "had not yet succeeded in taking control of the towns."
The Israeli military told AFP that more than 25 projectiles were launched from Lebanon towards Israel on Saturday, while air alert sirens sounded in the northern cities of Karmiel and Safed for the first time since the ceasefire, according to the army's Home Front Command.
Public broadcaster Kan aired footage shared on social media showing rockets falling into the sea off Israel's Nahariya, near the border, sending beachgoers fleeing.
Netanyahu announced on Friday that Israeli forces had advanced beyond the Litani River, which runs around 30 kilometres (20 miles) north of the Lebanon-Israel frontier, and were "hitting Hizbollah head on."
The Lebanese health ministry says that Israeli attacks have killed more than 3,371 people since March 2, when Hizbollah drew Lebanon into the Middle East war in support of its backer Iran.
Hizbollah said it attacked Israel in retaliation for the death of Iran's supreme leader in US-Israeli strikes when the war erupted on Feb 28.
Iran has insisted that any agreement to end the wider Middle East war also cover Lebanon.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Meteor travelling at 120,000kph explodes over US, shaking homes across region
Article:
WASHINGTON: A meteor crashing toward Earth exploded over the northeastern United States on Saturday, NASA said, setting off booms that echoed over the region with a blast equivalent to 300 tons of TNT.
The fireball broke up over northeastern Massachusetts and southeastern New Hampshire at 2.06pm (1806 GMT), the US space agency's deputy news chief Jennifer Dooren told AFP in a statement.
"This fireball was not associated with any currently active meteor shower, but it was a natural object and not a re-entry of space debris or a satellite," she said.
"The energy released at breakup is estimated to be equivalent to about 300 tons of TNT, which accounts for the loud booms."
The meteor was traveling at 75,000 mph (more than 120,000 kph) at an altitude of 40 miles when it broke apart, Dooren said.
Area residents were alarmed by the unexpected loud booms, with social media users reporting they were so powerful that houses were shaking.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US warns it is ready to resume Iran war as deal remains unresolved
Article:
WASHINGTON: The United States warned on Saturday it was "more than capable" of resuming its war with Iran after President Donald Trump said any peace deal must adhere to his red lines, including Teheran never being able to develop nuclear weapons.
The White House had signaled Trump was close to a decision on a potential deal, though Teheran denied there was a final agreement on ending the conflict.
US sources had told AFP the deal was waiting on Trump's sign-off, but he made no decision after a White House Situation Room meeting on Friday.
Pentagon chief Pete Hegseth, while attending a defence summit in Singapore, said on Saturday that Washington was "more than capable" of restarting the war if necessary, adding "our stockpiles are more than suited for that."
US Central Command (CENTCOM) posted on X that American forces "remain present and vigilant across the region."
Despite a ceasefire that has largely held since April, there have been occasional flare-ups.
Iran's IRNA state news agency said air defences shot down a drone "belonging to the US-Zionist aggressor enemy" on Saturday, citing the army.
Nevertheless diplomacy has continued, including to stop parallel fighting in Lebanon, which Iran has insisted be part of any deal to end the war and where Israeli forces have advanced further even as military delegations from both nations met at the Pentagon Friday.
Trump said his priorities for any deal included Iran agreeing to never develop nuclear weapons and the re-opening of the blockaded Strait of Hormuz.
"President Trump will only make a deal that is good for America and satisfies his red lines," a White House official told AFP, adding: "Iran can never possess a nuclear weapon."
- COMPETING CONDITIONS -
Iranian foreign ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baqaei pushed back on Trump's conditions, saying the Islamic republic "said goodbye to the language of 'must' 47 years ago."
Exchanges of messages were continuing, he added, but "no final agreement has been reached."
In his social media post, Trump said Teheran would remove mines from the Strait of Hormuz and end its closure of the waterway with "no tolls," while the US would lift its blockade of Iranian ports.
The two countries would also coordinate on removing and destroying Iran's enriched uranium, he said, adding that "no money will be exchanged, until further notice."
Iran's Fars news agency, however, cited sources as saying Teheran was demanding "the immediate release of US$12 billion" before moving to the next phase of negotiations.
And Iranian state television on Saturday said an "unofficial" draft memorandum of understanding said the United States agreed to release US$12 billion in frozen assets.
"The United States has pledged to provide Iran with full access to US$12 billion of its assets within 60 days, so that these resources can be transferred and spent in banks of Iran's desired destination without restrictions," the televised report said.
The White House has previously dismissed such claims as a "fabrication."
On the toll-free reopening of Hormuz, the sources cited by Fars news agency said "no such clause appears in the text of the agreement," while Trump's comment on destroying Iran's nuclear material "is fundamentally baseless."
Iran's ISNA news agency on Saturday cited lawmaker Alireza Salimi as saying a plan "to implement Iran's management and sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz will soon be approved by parliament."
Meanwhile, Iran's Tasnim news agency said the US blockade in the strait remained in place and its ships "are receiving warnings from CENTCOM to stop and not cross the blockade line."
- FIGHTING IN LEBANON -
Fighting has continued unabated on the war's Lebanese front, in spite of a separate ceasefire declared there.
Israel's military issued evacuation warnings for more villages south Lebanon on Saturday, a day after Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said Israeli forces had pushed more than 30 kilometres (20 miles) into the country.
Lebanese Prime Minister Nawaf Salam accused Israel of pursuing a "scorched-earth policy and collective punishment" in his country's south, and called for "a swift and real ceasefire."
A truce between Israel and Hizbollah began on April 17 but has never been observed, with both sides accusing each other of violating it.
In early March, Teheran-backed Hezbollah launched rockets at Israel over the US-Israeli killing of Iran's supreme leader, prompting Israeli strikes across Lebanon and a ground invasion.
Israel and Lebanon began direct talks in April, with a fourth round expected next week.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Online Safety Act 2025 a leap forward in protecting children
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: New safeguards to protect children from harmful online content will officially come into effect tomorrow, under the Online Safety Act 2025 (Onsa), marking a significant step in Malaysia's efforts to strengthen digital safety for minors.
Onsa was passed to address rising concerns over cyberbullying, online exploitation, harmful content and emerging digital threats affecting internet users.
The Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) announced on May 22 two new regulatory frameworks under the Act — the Child Protection Code (CPC) and the Risk Mitigation Code (RMC).
The codes place greater responsibility on online service providers and platforms to reduce digital harms, particularly those involving children and vulnerable users.
Under the CPC, platforms are required to adopt "child safety-by-design" measures, including safeguards to limit account registration and ownership by users under the age of 16.
Service providers are also expected to implement mechanisms to reduce children's exposure to exploitative interactions and harmful online content.
Meanwhile, the RMC requires platforms to establish proactive systems to identify and mitigate online risks.
These include conducting risk assessments, strengthening content governance, improving reporting and response systems, verifying advertisers, and labelling manipulated or synthetic content where appropriate.
[[nid:1451848]]
MCMC said the implementation of these codes will be outcome-based, giving platform providers flexibility to meet safety, privacy and legal requirements.
The commission also said a reasonable grace period would be provided to service providers to complete verification processes and comply with the new requirements.
The new framework was introduced following public consultations held between Feb 12 and March 31, involving industry players, civil society organisations and other stakeholders.
Content regulators have welcomed the move, describing it as a timely and necessary step amid growing concerns over the impact of digital platforms on young users.
Communications and Multimedia Content Forum of Malaysia chief executive officer Mediha Mahmood said the safeguards sent an important policy signal to parents and guardians on the risks children may face in digital spaces.
She said while measures to limit access for younger users could help reduce immediate exposure to online harms, digital safety should also be supported through continuous parental guidance, digital literacy and safer platform design.
"The last thing we want is for parents to become too comforted by this under-16 safeguard and think it has solved everything.
"As parents, you can never absolve yourselves of responsibility when it comes to children," she said.
Mediha said that the focus must remain on measures that are practical, proportionate and capable of addressing harms such as grooming, scams, fraud and cyberbullying.
She said platform providers should enable built-in protections for children by default, rather than relying on parents to activate them manually.
"There shouldn't be a whole process where parents have to search for parental locks and safety features and switch them on. It should already be on by default," she said.
[[nid:1451847]]
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: AI deepens online threats to children [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Before artificial intelligence dominated the tech world, the Internet was already dangerous for children.
Experts warn that AI has supercharged online threats to children, as harmful content can be designed to evade moderation algorithms.
In response to these rapidly evolving dangers, they believe that soon-to-be-enforced social media guidelines for minors — including age limits and clear platform accountability — are necessary and overdue to secure digital safety.
Sarah Yong Li Hsien, chairperson of the AI, Cyberlaw and Data Protection Committee at the Malaysian Bar, said the problem stems from the widespread availability of content designed to evade scrutiny.
[[nid:1451843]]
"As bad actors become more creative, parents must be increasingly vigilant about how content can be interpreted in harmful ways," she said.
She added that children are particularly susceptible due to their developmental stage and limited digital literacy.
"Bad actors have made technology so intuitive that a child does not need to be taught how to use it; they just know," she said.
She said Malaysia's existing laws offer some protection, but they have limits.
"Technologies evolve rapidly, but laws do not. Legislation must focus on outcomes rather than specific technologies to allow for future innovation and cover crimes yet to be created," she said.
Currently, Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998 and the forthcoming Online Safety Act (Onsa) 2025 provide pathways to remove harmful content.
A Cybercrime Bill that targets AI-generated child sexual abuse material is being drafted.
However, she admitted legislation will always be "playing catch-up".
Regarding the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission's (MCMC) proposal to set a minimum age of 16 for social media access, the Malaysian Bar voiced cautions.
"Child online safety measures should not automatically result in a blanket exclusionary approach for those under 16," she warned, noting it could infringe on constitutional rights to privacy and expression, which are provided for under the United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child.
The Bar also drew a sharp distinction between age verification for safety and mandatory identity disclosure for all users. The former, she said, is acceptable while the latter is not.
Onsa is a paradigm shift, placing clearer duties on platform providers to protect children rather than leaving the burden solely on parents.
She noted that while platforms are subjected to the Communications and Multimedia Act and the Personal Data Protection Act 2010 (PDPA), the new Onsa establishes an explicit accountability framework.
"Where responsibilities are imposed, they should be accompanied by clear legal standards, transparency, appropriate safeguards and accountable oversight mechanisms, rather than relying solely on voluntary measures," she said, calling for transparent oversight rather than a reliance on voluntary measures.
She also called for amendments to the PDPA, which currently excludes the federal and state governments and is limited to personal data in commercial transactions. This leaves children's data processed for the purposes of age-verification potentially unprotected.
Professor Dr Ainuddin Wahid Abdul Wahab of Universiti Malaya's Centre of Research for Cybersecurity and Network compares online safety with teaching a child to cross the road.
"You don't need to understand traffic engineering; you just need to hold their hand," he said, advocating for open communication and shared devices.
Professor Dr Selvakumar Manickam, director of Universiti Sains Malaysia's Cybersecurity Research Centre, warns that because criminals bypass filters faster than laws are written, education is the only "durable protection".
He said that equipping children with digital literacy and critical thinking skills is vital.
Selvakumar said a child who is taught to question the source and intent of online content is far harder to manipulate.
"They must question who built the content and why, to resist grooming and manipulation.
"A digitally literate child carries a defence no platform can switch off," he said, adding that this literacy must extend to parents to strengthen the chain of protection.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Dozens arrested in Paris clashes on PSG victory night
Article:
PARIS: Police on Saturday arrested dozens of people in clashes that erupted in the French capital as thousands poured onto the streets during Paris Saint-Germain's Champions League final victory.
Some 22,000 police were deployed across France for the game, including 8,000 in Paris, after unrest marred PSG's win in the competition last year. Paris tram lines were halted, several metro stations shut and bus traffic halted in places in bid to minimise disturbances.
According to Paris police, 79 people were detained in the city, of whom 45 were taken into custody. Six vehicles and two businesses were damaged. A police officer was also injured.
As fans celebrated the dramatic penalty shoot out victory, some 20,000 people converged on the city's iconic Champs-Elysees avenue, police said.
Shops boarded up their windows ahead of the match to avoid a repeat of disturbances last year when youths ransacked shops on the Champs-Elysees and other streets. Hundreds of people were arrested.
Two dozen flares and about 100 fireworks were seized Saturday, while a bus shelter was destroyed near the Champs-Elysees avenue.
But fireworks rang out across Paris after the French side completed their win in Budapest.
Police said a bakery and a restaurant were damaged near PSG's Parc des Princes stadium, where tens of thousands gathered inside to watch the match but 4,000- 5,000 people loitered outside with projectiles were thrown at officers.
About 150 people "attempted to enter through one of the gates" at the stadium but police pushed them back, a police spokesperson said.
Some also attempted to erect a barricade with rental bikes which was cleared by police.
An AFP reporter at the scene said clashes broke out between police and supporters near the stadium, and officers responded with tear gas when fireworks were thrown at them.
Interior Minister Laurent Nunez said there was a "very robust, very solid system in place" to curb violence.
"Our responsibility is to guarantee everyone a festive celebration that is calm and fully secure," a police spokesperson said.
Last year, supporters ransacked shops on the Champs -Elysees and other streets after the PSG win, with hundreds of people arrested. --AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Teenager dies after motorcycle collides with car in Klang
Article:
SHAH ALAM: A teenage boy, 18, was killed in an accident involving two cars and a motorcycle along Jalan Kebun, Klang, here yesterday morning.
In the 10.05am incident, the victim, who was riding a motorcycle from the Shah Alam Expressway (Kesas) towards the Jalan Kebun roundabout, is believed to have attempted to overtake a car before colliding with it.
Klang South district police chief, Assistant Commissioner Lim Jit Huey, said the impact caused the victim to be thrown into the opposite lane, where he was then hit by another car that was unable to avoid him.
"As a result of the impact, the victim sustained severe injuries to his head and body and was confirmed dead at the scene," he said in a statement today.
He said the victim's remains were sent to the Forensic Department of Tengku Ampuan Rahimah Hospital (HTAR), Klang, for post-mortem examination.
Lim said both car drivers involved were unhurt. The case is being investigated under Section 41(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987.
He urged members of the public with information or who witnessed the incident to come forward and assist investigations by contacting the nearest police station.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Queen's recent trip marks latest chapter in close Malaysia–Türkiye relations
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Her Majesty Raja Zarith Sofiah, Queen of Malaysia's recent departure to Türkiye marks the latest chapter in the close bilateral relations between Malaysia and Türkiye.
According to a post on Sultan Ibrahim Sultan Iskandar's Facebook page, the close relationship between Malaysia and Türkiye has seen strong cooperation in defence, trade and international Islamic diplomacy.
The relationship between the state of Johor and Türkiye has its own unique history.
"The Modern Johor Sultanate, founded by the late Sultan Abu Bakar in the mid-19th century, was greatly influenced by foreign administrative systems due to international diplomatic relations, which became one of His Majesty's extraordinary skills," the post said.
The statement also said that British influence was applied to the governance and administration system of Johor as a result of Sultan Abu Bakar's close friendship with Queen Victoria.
However, there was another significant influence that inspired the shift towards modernisation of the Johor administration at the time.
As an Islamic kingdom, the Johor Sultanate also established close relations with the Ottoman Empire in Istanbul, which was undergoing the "Tanzimat" modernisation era at the time.
Sultan Abu Bakar travelled to Istanbul twice, first in 1879 and again in 1893, where he was received with recognition and full respect.
According to the statement, Johor used Istanbul as a model to understand the development of modernisation under Western colonial pressure, while maintaining cultural autonomy and Malay-Islamic identity.
The governance and administration of the Ottoman Empire made significant contributions to the formation of the government and legal system of the modern Johor Sultanate through the Ahkam Johor magazine.
It also influenced the drafting of the Johor State Constitution of 1895, the first written constitution enacted in Malaya.
The Johor state flag, which is still in use today and was designed by Sultan Abu Bakar in 1871, also reflects the influence and close friendship with the Ottoman Empire, and became a strategic symbol of Johor's sovereignty at the time against Western colonial powers.
"This relationship has grown deeper than just diplomatic ties, as it has also developed through family links and the sharing of cultural heritage that can still be seen today," the statement added.
According to the post, the statement was adapted from the writings of the director of the Graduate School and professor at the Department of History, Faculty of Arts, Istanbul Medeniyet University, Prof Dr Ismail Hakki Kadi, and Dr Yakoob Ahmed, independent researchers and former associate professors at the Institute of Islamic Studies, Istanbul University.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: KL Festival 2026 concludes, Asian Music Games to take centre stage in September
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Jalan Raja came alive with marching band performances as the Kuala Lumpur Festival 2026 drew to a spectacular close.
The evening also marked the official launch of the Asian Music Games (AMG) Kuala Lumpur 2026, scheduled from Sept 4 to 13, which will bring together marching bands, orchestras and music performers from across Asia in an Olympic-style competition format.
In her speech, Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Federal Territories) Hannah Yeoh said the federal government had invested heavily in upgrading the Sultan Abdul Samad Building (BSAS) and other nearby heritage structures, but cautioned that buildings alone meant little without the people who gave them meaning.
"These buildings mean nothing if we do not have the people celebrating them, the rich heritage that we have, and the potential that this city has to offer.
"This space would just be a beautiful building with beautiful lights, but it is the people, the bands, the parents, the supporters and the sponsors who have made this come alive," she said at the closing ceremony on the boulevard between Dataran Merdeka and BSAS here today.
Also in attendance were Kuala Lumpur Mayor Datuk Fadlun Mak Ujud, Think City senior director Izan Satrina Mohd Sallehuddin and Asian Marching Band Confederation (AMBC) president Sehat Kurniawan Saiman.
Yeoh called on Kuala Lumpur City Hall (DBKL) and Think City to continue organising programmes that bring together Malaysians from all walks of life to use the space.
[[nid:1451808]]
"Whether it is arts, culture, sports or education — this space belongs to all of us," she said.
Izan said KL Festival 2026, which took place from May 6 to 31, quickly grew beyond expectations as communities, businesses, artists and volunteers stepped forward.
"Every ringgit spent by the government helped unlock even greater participation and support from the wider ecosystem, generating more than RM8 million in partnership value and a multiplier effect of more than three times the original investment," she said.
Meanwhile, AMG Kuala Lumpur 2026, carrying the theme "Music of Us", is expected to feature 105 medal events with an estimated 7,000 local and 3,000 international participants, of whom around 80 per cent are students.
Competition will be held across three age categories — Junior, Senior and Open — with participation expected from nine countries: Malaysia, Indonesia, Thailand, Singapore, the Philippines, Taiwan, Sri Lanka, India and Japan.
AMG Kuala Lumpur 2026 is the second edition of the games, following the inaugural event held in Jember, Indonesia in 2023.
The national selection process is already under way through the Malaysia National Band Competition (Natcomp) 2026, which serves as the official qualifier for the regional-level games.
Previously, Yeoh said
KL Festival would be held annually
beginning May next year in support of Visit Malaysia 2027 and to strengthen Kuala Lumpur's standing as a Unesco Creative City, as part of efforts to transform the nation's capital into a vibrant cultural destination beyond its commercial identity.
The 26-day festival featured more than 80 events and over 700 hours of programming across 25 venues and public spaces in downtown Kuala Lumpur, showcasing reimagined wayang kulit performances, contemporary dance, immersive installations, outdoor cinema and theatre productions, including Malaysian, Asian and international premieres.
The cultural celebration was brought to life through the collective efforts of artists, communities and partners across the city, including Media Prima, Yayasan Hasanah, TSLaw Group, Epson, KLCC (Holdings), PNB Merdeka Ventures, Serai Group, AirAsia Move, Grab and many others.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Legacy of Himalaya's 'Sherlock Holmes' lives on in digital
Article:
IN a crowded Kathmandu restaurant far from Nepal's famed icy peaks, Billi Bierling questions climbers about their ascents, preserving each triumph in the Himalayan Database, mountaineering's revered 60-year-old register of success.
German climber Bierling, 58, inherited the stewardship of the archive from her mentor, the late Elizabeth Hawley, an American journalist who began the post-expedition interviews in Nepal while covering an American Everest expedition in 1963.
"It was her fascination," Bierling told AFP, after interviewing a Russian and a Ukrainian climber about their ascent of Manaslu, the world's eighth-highest mountain.
"She never climbed," Bierling said. "She never even went to a base camp – but the people interested her."
Hawley's 50 years of chronicling climbs in the Himalayas earned her the moniker "the Sherlock Holmes of the mountaineering world" from Edmund Hillary, who with Tenzing Norgay made the first summit of Everest.
By the time she passed away in 2018, she had built a reputation as one of the most authoritative voices on Himalayan mountaineering.
The database she began had become the definitive record of Himalayan expeditions – used by climbers, historians and researchers alike.
"She was very, very keen on her data, on her information," said Bierling, who first met Hawley in 2001 – when she was in Nepal to climb the 7,129-metre (23,390-foot) Baruntse – and began assisting her in 2004.
She described how Hawley would give the same grilling to all – whether a climbing legend or an unknown.
"It didn't matter whether you were Reinhold Messner or you were Ueli Steck," she said, referring to the Italian great who made the first solo ascent of Everest, as well as the late Swiss speed climber.
"Or if you were Billi Bierling, a nobody," she said with a smile.
Bierling is now part of the team that continues her work, updating the vast database year after year.
At a time when each year more climbers are attempting the world's highest peaks than ever before, the task of recording the ascents is even more important.
But times have changed.
In the 1970s, Hawley would drive to Kathmandu's airport in her blue Volkswagen Beetle, spotting those carrying the tell-tale heavy mountaineering boots as they walked off the two or three international flights a week.
Soon, it was the mountaineers who would seek her out.
In 1991, American climber Richard Salisbury, recognising the archive's historical importance and fragility, proposed digitising it.
It took nearly 11 years, Salisbury told AFP, a painstaking effort to convert "nearly 40 full file drawers" of meticulous handwritten expedition reports into a searchable digital resource.
[[nid:1451794]]
"It was very important for a mountaineer to have their summit recorded in the Himalayan Database," said Garrett Madison, who has organised expeditions in Nepal since the 2000s, speaking to AFP from Everest base camp.
"If it wasn't recorded, it didn't happen."
Japanese climber Tatsuro Sugimoto, in Kathmandu after completing the first ascent of the 6,473-metre (21,237-foot) Jarkya, said the database was key for mountaineers seeking new records and routes.
"It is useful; we can check which mountains are unclimbed," he said.
Its scale, like climbing, has expanded exponentially.
Commercial expeditions now send hundreds of climbers each season, some summiting more than one peak.
"At one point I thought, 'this is no longer possible'," Bierling said. "I would be only running around. More and more mountains were being commercialised."
The database now supplements its records with official expedition numbers from Nepal's Ministry of Tourism.
"If we wanted to meet everybody in person, we'd need an army of 100 people," Bierling said. "It's all so quick. People come and go, they fly in, they fly out."
Nepal has issued a record 492 Everest permits for foreigners this season, with a city of tents set up at the foot of the mountain for climbers and support staff.
The focus has shifted to significant ascents, including first summits or new routes that push boundaries.
"The interesting climbs we chase," she said.
But the database still follows one of Hawley's key principles: first, trust the climber.
"Only when doubt arises do we dig in and look into it," said volunteer Tobias Pantel, 39.
"Then we can check the topography in their photographs and if other climbers are contradicting the claims."
As a result, some climbs are tagged "disputed", said Bierling.
"I often wonder what would Miss Hawley say," she said.
* The writer is from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UN places Israel and Russia on sexual violence blacklist
Article:
NEW YORK: The United Nations on Friday added Israel and Russia to a UN blacklist of countries suspected of committing sexual violence in conflict zones, a move that prompted Israel's foreign ministry to say it would sever all ties with UN Secretary-General Antonio Guterres.
Guterres' annual report to the UN Security Council on conflict-related sexual violence goes a step further than last year, when he put Israel and Russia "on notice" that they could be added to the list of parties "credibly suspected of committing or being responsible for patterns of rape or other forms of sexual violence."
The latest report does that and contains harrowing descriptions of abuses at the hands of Israeli and Russian armed and security forces.
Israel's arch enemy Hamas, whose Oct 7, 2023, attack on southern Israel triggered the war in Gaza, was already on the blacklist.
Being added to the list does not automatically carry specific punitive measures such as sanctions, although public naming and shaming can cause significant reputational damage for the states involved, and those repeatedly listed are barred from UN peacekeeping operations.
The report's compiler, Pramila Patten, Guterres' special representative on sexual violence in conflict, confirmed at a news briefing that there had been an invitation from Israel for a visit, but referred also to disagreements about the scope of the visit and related issues of access and cooperation, and said it ultimately had to be suspended due to the war in Gaza.
She said cases of conflict-related sexual violence verified by the United Nations globally rose by more than 100 per cent in 2025 over 2024 and called it a very disturbing trend that was still only the "very tip of the iceberg."
This year's report said that in 2025, "the United Nations verified multiple incidents of conflict-related sexual violence, including as a form of torture, inflicted against 14 men, seven women, nine boys and one girl from the Gaza Strip and the West Bank."
It said 13 of the cases occurred in 2025, and 18 in 2023 and 2024.
"Violations consisted of rape, including with objects, gang rape, attempted rape, physical violence to the genitals, instances of targeted shooting of the genitals, touching of breasts and genitals, strip and cavity searches conducted without apparent security justification, forced nudity and threats of rape," it said.
"Rape and gang rape, in some cases repeated, were perpetrated against nine victims, the majority from Gaza," it said, adding that perpetrators included Israeli armed and security forces and occurred primarily during detention and interrogation and across several sites, including military camps and also at checkpoints and during Israeli military operations in the Occupied Palestinian Territory.
It said survivors included journalists and human rights defenders and that in some cases, the violations were filmed or photographed, including one case of rape.
The report added that sexual violence against female detainees included mostly threats of rape, forced nudity, unwanted touching, and humiliating or degrading strip searches without justification, while men and boys were targeted with rape, attempted rape and violence to the genitals.
This resulted in five male victims suffering severe rectal bleeding or swelling for multiple days or weeks, it said.
The report said the U.N. human rights monitoring mission in Ukraine had verified 310 cases of conflict-related sexual violence perpetrated by Russian armed and security forces.
It said the cases, including rape, gang rape, genital mutilation, electric shocks and beatings to the genitals, affected 280 men, 26 women and four girls.
In a post on X on Thursday, Israel's UN Ambassador Danny Danon said ranking Israel with the resistance group marked a "new low."
"This is a political decision! Disconnected from the facts and reality!" Danon said in another post by the Israeli mission to the UN which said he was informed about it during a phone call with Guterres.
Russia's UN mission did not immediately respond to a request for comment on the report, which Ukraine's foreign minister, Andrii Sybiha, welcomed in a post on X.
The US ambassador to the United Nations, Mike Waltz, posted on X that it was "ridiculous for the UN to put a democracy like Israel — with robust rule of law that conducts investigations and holds criminals accountable — on the same level as Hamas."
Danon said Israel had responded in detail to each allegation and had invited UN representatives to visit and examine the situation, but that they had chosen not to do so.
"Given that Antonio Guterres has chosen to violate every standard of honesty, integrity and professionalism, Israel has decided to sever all ties with the Secretary-General's Office and will wait until a new UN Secretary-General is appointed," Israel's Foreign Ministry posted on X.
A new UN secretary-general is due to be appointed later this year.
Asked about Danon's comments at a regular briefing on Thursday, UN spokesperson Stephane Dujarric said: "From the Secretary-General's point of view, his door remains open to Israeli representatives, as to the other 192 member states and the two observer states."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Singaporean man injured in fall at rocky section of Gunung Datuk
Article:
SEREMBAN: A Singaporean man was injured after allegedly falling at a rocky section while descending Gunung Datuk in Rembau near here this morning.
Kota Fire and Rescue Station chief, Senior Fire Officer II Asnizam Kamberon, said the department received a call regarding the incident involving the 52-year-old victim at 10.29am.
"The victim was among 11 hikers taking part in a climb to the summit of Gunung Datuk, accompanied by two Negri Sembilan Forestry Mountain Guides.
"He is believed to have fallen at a rocky area near the summit staircase while descending the mountain and suffered a fracture to his right arm," he told Bernama.
He said the Operations Response Team hiked to the victim's location, about two kilometres from the foot of the mountain, and brought him down using a stretcher.
Asnizam said the victim was subsequently handed over to medical personnel for further treatment, and the operation involving five personnel concluded at 2.28pm.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Genting crash: Bus driver with 29 traffic summonses arrested over reckless driving
Article:
BENTONG: A 55-year-old bus driver, who has 29 outstanding traffic summonses, has been arrested for alleged reckless driving that led to a three-vehicle crash at Km16 of the Genting Highlands Road here today.
Bentong district police chief, Superintendent Zaiham Mohd Kahar, said police received a report on the incident at 5.09pm involving two express buses and a Perodua Aruz MPV travelling towards the capital.
"The first bus is believed to have lost control at a bend before hitting the rear of the Perodua Aruz and subsequently colliding with the rear of another express bus.
"Due to the impact, the second express bus lost control, skidded and overturned on the left side of the road, while the first bus veered to the right side of the road," he said in a statement today.
He said the two buses were carrying 38 and 36 passengers respectively, including foreign workers from an electronics factory in Selangor who were returning home after a trip to Genting Highlands.
He said initial investigations found that the crash was caused by the driver of the first express bus, who was believed to have been driving recklessly.
"The case is being investigated under Section 43(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987.
"The bus driver has been detained to assist investigations, and a urine screening test conducted on him returned negative," he said.
Zaiham said checks also revealed that the driver had 29 outstanding traffic summonses for various offences.
He said no fatalities were reported in the incident, but five bus passengers comprising three Bangladeshis, two Nepalis and a Malaysian MPV passenger sustained minor injuries.
All six injured victims were sent to Selayang Hospital and Kuala Lumpur Hospital for further treatment.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Poison? More artists flee Trump-linked US anniversary concerts
Article:
WASHINGTON: President Donald Trump's troubled bid for a star-studded musical celebration of the 250th anniversary of US independence took yet another hit Friday after three more acts pulled out citing political tensions.
Trump's administration had publicised multiple headliners for a "Freedom 250" concert series between June 25 and July 10 on the National Mall in Washington.
But the event is floundering as one act after another withdraws.
On Friday, glam rocker Bret Michaels and country star Martina McBride joined the Commodores and two other groups announcing they would not take part.
This left only four of the line-up originally announced on Wednesday intact.
Those include Vanilla Ice, C+C Music Factory, Flo Rida, and the surviving member of the duo Milli Vanilli, who are best known for a lip-syncing controversy more than three decades ago.
Michaels, frontman of 1980s band Poison – of "Every Rose Has Its Thorn" fame – said Friday on Facebook that he made a "difficult decision to step away from this performance."
"My shows have never been about politics. They're about giving people a place to come together, have a great time and forget about life's stresses for a few hours," he said.
"Unfortunately, what was presented to us as a celebration of our country has evolved into something much more divisive than what I agreed to be a part of."
A White House official said that "this is not a White House event" when asked by AFP about the withdrawals, and referred inquiries to the "Freedom 250" celebration organisers.
"Freedom 250" insists that it is a nonpartisan and non-political organisation.
It describes itself as a public-private partnership that works with a White House task force for the anniversary. Trump set up the task force last year to deliver the celebrations for this summer.
[[nid:1451758]]
But Trump has openly tried to stamp his mark on the celebrations, most notably by staging an ultra-violent mixed martial arts fight in a specially constructed arena on the White House lawn on June 14 – his 80th birthday.
Award-winning country singer McBride announced she too will not be performing at the "Great American State Fair" next month.
"I was presented with an opportunity to perform at a nonpartisan event but that turned out to be misleading," she told her fans in a post on X.
They join several other acts abandoning the programme or dismissing rumours they would perform: rapper Young MC, best known for 1989's smash hit "Bust a Move;" musician Morris Day and the Time, who once collaborated with Prince; and Grammy-winning soul group the Commodores.
"The Commodores will not be performing" at the event, the band posted late Thursday on Instagram. "We support the betterment of all Americans."
But Vanilla Ice, known for the 1990 hit "Ice Ice Baby", insisted he would be playing.
"I'm not going to get dragged into that. I'm here to party with America, man," the performer told TMZ – adding that he would also play for President Joe Biden, Russian leader Vladimir Putin or in Iran if asked.
With artists fleeing, the Democratic Party on Friday trolled Trump's event, posting an image of the nine announced performers, with Xs over the five cancelled acts.
By contrast, on Wednesday night the nation's capital hosted singer Bruce Springsteen, a fierce Trump opponent, who announced he would return to the Washington area on Oct 3 for the Power to the People Festival.
That show will include Foo Fighters, Dave Matthews, Joan Baez, Public Enemy, and other top-billing acts.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Chow extends condolences to family of man killed in Odeon building fire
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Chief Minister Chow Kon Yeow has extended his deepest condolences to the family of a man who died in a fire at the Midnight Black entertainment outlet, formerly the Odeon Theatre, along Penang Road here tonight.
Chow said the Fire and Rescue Department had confirmed one fatality in the incident as of tonight.
"Upon receiving an alert on the fire incident at Midnight Black, I immediately rushed to the scene together with Tanjung member of parliament Lim Hui Ying to assess the situation and receive updates from the relevant authorities," he said after arriving at the scene at about 9.40pm.
Chow said firefighters acted swiftly to bring the blaze under control within a short period, preventing it from spreading to adjacent buildings, including those along Sri Bahari Road.
He said preliminary information indicated that the premises had not commenced operations when the fire broke out.
"The cause of the incident and the extent of losses will be determined following further investigations by the Fire and Rescue Department," he added.
Chow also expressed appreciation to firefighters and emergency responders for their professionalism and prompt action in containing the fire and safeguarding nearby residents and properties.
"I would like to express my sincere appreciation to all firefighters and emergency responders for their professionalism and swift action in safeguarding nearby residents and properties," he said.
The fire at the Penang Road premises broke out on Saturday night, with authorities still investigating the cause of the incident.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: One killed in fire at Penang's historic Odeon building
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: One person was killed in a fire that broke out at the former Odeon Cinema building along Penang Road here tonight.
It is understood that the building, which currently houses the Midnight Black nightclub, was closed for renovation works.
Penang Fire and Rescue Department director Mohamad Shoki Hamzah confirmed that one person had died, believed to have been trapped in the blaze.
"We can confirm that one person is dead," he told the New Straits Times when contacted.
It was reported that
a fire broke out at the former Odeon Cinema
building along Penang Road tonight, sending firefighters rushing to contain the blaze at one of the state's most recognisable heritage landmarks.
Meanwhile, the Penang Fire and Rescue Department's operations centre said it received an emergency call at 8.50pm.
Firefighters from the Lebuh Pantai fire and rescue station, assisted by personnel from the Jalan Perak, Bayan Baru and Paya Terubong stations, were immediately dispatched to the scene.
The first fire engine arrived at 9pm and found the blaze involving a two-storey shoplot measuring about 15m by 91m
Initial assessments indicated that about 50 per cent of the building had been damaged by the fire.
Firefighters are carrying out containment and extinguishing operations with assistance from supporting units.
A total of 25 personnel, including supervising officers, are involved in the operation, supported by six fire engines, an Emergency Medical Rescue Services (EMRS) unit and an Aerial Ladder Platform (ALP).
Firefighting operations were still ongoing at press time, with updates to be provided as more information becomes available.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: What if the AI boom goes into reverse?
Article:
INVESTORS should be asking not whether the AI boom will end, but what happens to markets when it does, and where safety may be found.
I have previously argued that the business models of OpenAI, Anthropic and other AI darlings look increasingly unsustainable, and that hyperscalers such as Meta and Amazon face daunting maths in making their data centre investments pay off.
Yet for now, the investment surge continues to accelerate. Annual investment in technology equipment and software reached US$1.5 trillion last year — about 70 per cent more than during the inflation-adjusted peak of the late-1990s dotcom boom.
While I do not expect the AI boom to stop abruptly, growth will eventually stall. Every major tech boom since World War  2 has been followed by a pullback in investment.
After the 2000 TMT bubble burst, for example, tech investment fell 18.6 per cent over two years, according to the Bureau of Economic Analysis.
Given the scale of today's spending, even a modest slowdown could have global consequences.
My models suggest that even a five per cent drop in US tech investment could knock up to one percentage point off real gross domestic product in the United States, United Kingdom and euro zone in the following year.
To assess risk, I modelled three possible endings to the AI capex boom: a mild correction, a normal recessionary downturn and a full TMT-style bust.
In a mild correction of around 4.5 per cent, US equities could fall roughly 15 per cent, while European markets could drop more than 20 per cent.
In a typical tech recession with a six per cent investment decline, losses could exceed 20 per cent in the US and 30 per cent in Europe, potentially triggering broader economic recession as investors repatriate capital.
In a full TMT-style crash, drawdowns could exceed 50 per cent.
A reversal would  hit European equities harder on average, but some of the best relative "safe havens" may still be in Europe.
European pharmaceuticals and food sectors could also outperform US equivalents. The reason is flow-driven: in a downturn, US investors tend to repatriate capital and sell broader holdings, amplifying global equity weakness, including in defensive sectors. European defensives would likely face less of that forced selling pressure.
While the AI boom may appear unstoppable, it will eventually lose momentum. Investors should prepare now rather than react in panic when the cycle turns.
Writing for Reuters, the writer is an investment strategist for Panmure Liberum
The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Investigative journalism is indispensable
Article:
IN an era marked by complex financial crimes, abuse of authority and institutional corruption, investigative journalism has become more important than ever.
Through objective, professional reporting, investigative journalists help drive action from authorities and encourage transparency in government insti-tutions.
One of the most influential examples of investigative journalism was the 2016 Panama Papers investigation coordinated  by the International Consortium of Investigative Journalists.
The global investigation involved over 370 journalists across 76 countries working in 25 languages. It exposed how wealthy individuals, politicians and business figures used offshore companies and tax havens to conceal assets and avoid taxes.
Among the 143 politicians named were several individuals linked to Malaysia.
The investigation underscored  how international collaboration can expose large-scale financial and institutional corruption.
It also highlighted the growing importance of cross-border collaboration between journalists, data analysts and digital investigators in tracking hidden financial networks.
In Malaysia, corruption involving bribery, embezzlement, procurement  and abuse of power  dominate headlines.
The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) said more than    6,600 people were detained for corruption offences between 2020 and 2026,  and over RM16.29 billion in assets, settlements, forfeitures, and compounds was recovered  in the same period.
MACC data also revealed that more than 40,000 complaints were received between 2020 and 2025, resulting in thousands of investigations, arrests and prosecutions.
Procurement remains one of the sectors most vulnerable to corruption, often involving collusion, inflated contracts, abuse of authority and embezzlement of public funds.
The 1Malaysia Development Bhd  case  demonstrated the devastating impact of institutional corruption.
It showed how investigative reporting, local and global, played a significant role in exposing financial misconduct linked to political elites.
Investigative journalism in Malaysia has played a significant role in exposing  human rights violations and threats to public safety.
In 2015, investigative reports revealed the existence of mass graves and human trafficking camps in Wang Kelian near Malaysia's northern border, prompting the government to establish a royal commission of inquiry to examine the incident.
Likewise, media investigations into a  syndicate  allegedly importing non-halal meat and falsely certifying it as halal generated nationwide concern.
The case also highlighted possible misconduct and corruption involving enforcement authorities.
More recent corruption probes have revealed the enormous scale of illicit wealth linked to abuse of power.
In 2025 and 2026, investigations uncovered luxury vehicles, gold bars, millions of ringgit in cash and high-end properties tied to corruption and money- laundering schemes.
These cases illustrate how corruption not only drains public resources but also widens inequality and weakens confidence in public institutions.
Despite  their critical role in promoting accountability and transparency, journalists worldwide are increasingly subjected to intimidation, censorship, online harassment and legal pressures intended to suppress independent reporting and discourage scrutiny of those in  power.
According to Reporters Without Borders, global press freedom in 2026 declined to its lowest level in 25 years, driven  by increasing political control, restrictive legislation and mounting pressure on media organisations and journalists.
Malaysia has nevertheless shown some improvement in media freedom.
In the 2025 World Press Freedom Index, Malaysia climbed 19 places to  88th globally after previously dropping to 107th in 2024.
The Human Rights Commission of Malaysia (Suhakam) has repeatedly stressed the importance of protecting freedom of expression and ensuring journalism can operate without fear or favour.
These protections are essential because investigative journalists depend  on whistleblowers and confidential sources.
Without legal safeguards, many individuals may hesitate to expose corruption involving powerful figures.
At the same time, journalists themselves must uphold high ethical standards.
Strong investigative reporting not only informs the public but also assists law enforcement agencies by providing  information that may accelerate investigations.
The future of investigative journalism will also depend on innovation and specialised skills.
Modern corruption schemes  involve digital transactions, offshore banking, cryptocurrency and sophisticated financial networks.
As a result, journalists must  adapt by developing expertise in data analysis, cyber investigations and digital research tools.
Younger journalists are well positioned to leverage technology and data-driven investiga-
tive methods to uncover corruption.
Ultimately, investigative journalism remains one of society's most effective weapons against systemic corruption.
The writer is professor and director at the Institute of Criminology and Criminal Justice, HELP University
The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NST Leader: Are our rail ambitions on track?
Article:
INVESTIGATORS looking into the Ampang/Sri Petaling light rail transit (LRT) train derailment near the Chan Sow Lin station on Thursday, will likely point to one of three vulnerabilities: human error, mechanical ageing (the LRT is almost 30 years old) or software glitches.
Nevertheless, the Transport Ministry has commendably tasked the Air Accident Investigation Bureau with leading the probe into the incident. The task force has 30 days to examine all aspects of LRT operations. Hopefully the probe will ignite some needed long-term reforms.
Since its inception, the LRT has suffered hundreds of minor breakdowns and component failures, but only a handful of major collisions and derailments. Besides the recent derailment, currently blamed on a faulty track switch, there have been several major incidents.
In 2021, an autonomous empty train on the Kelana Jaya line collided with another one carrying 213 passengers between the Kampung Baru and KLCC stations. Forty-seven passengers were seriously injured, and 166 received outpatient treatment. In 2008, a train rear-ended another train, resulting in minor injuries among the 300 passengers.
That stated, our LRT isn't exactly the worst but it lags in platform safety infrastructure and automatic gates, leaving the tracks vulnerable to track intrusions and passengers vulnerable to accidental falls.
Still, the recent incident raises pertinent questions. The regular mishaps can be attributed to a "build first, maintain later" policy, scrambling RapidKL to overcome aging systems, track deterioration, obsolete signalling systems and component fatigue. Was there a rigorous preventive maintenance regime? And is the equipment up to date or long overdue for replacement?
This derailment should compel policymakers to confront whether budgets and expertise have kept up with Malaysia's rail ambitions. Undoubtedly, this will demand funding, political will and institutional commitment, especially to mandatory public disclosures on safety and maintenance.
Perhaps Prasarana Malaysia Bhd should study how public transport systems globally treat maintenance as a national priority, particularly London's Underground: it may have a bigger accident record being 163-years-old, but its modern safety systems have kept a lid on major crashes.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: DOSH orders work halt after Indonesian worker killed in wall collapse
Article:
audreymd@nst.com.my
BUKIT MERTAJAM: The Penang Department of Occupational Safety and Health (DOSH) has ordered an immediate halt to all work activities at the site where an Indonesian worker was killed after being crushed by a wall at Jalan Guar Perahu Indah here yesterday.
The order follows the completion of preliminary investigations into the incident.
In a statement tonight, Penang DOSH said several witnesses and representatives of the employer had been instructed to appear at its office to assist in further investigations.
"We are investigating a fatal workplace accident involving a male worker who is believed to have been crushed by a wall while carrying out demolition works at a premises undergoing renovation.
"The incident was reported at 3pm yesterday when the victim and several other workers were demolishing a wall in the building's stairway area.
"The victim was pronounced dead at the scene after suffering severe injuries," it said.
It was reported that the Indonesian construction worker was killed after being crushed by falling concrete at a construction site in Jalan Guar Perahu Indah here.
The victim, identified only as Marzuki, 27, was
carrying out construction work
when the incident occurred on the ground floor of the premises.
Initial investigations found that renovation works at the premises were being carried out by a contractor appointed to undertake demolition and interior refurbishment works.
Penang DOSH said the worker was using demolition equipment to break down part of the wall structure when it suddenly collapsed and struck him.
It added that investigations are being conducted under the Occupational Safety and Health Act 1994 (Act 514) to determine the cause of the incident and whether there had been any breaches of occupational safety and health regulations.
"DOSH reminds all employers, contractors and parties in control of workplaces to carry out adequate risk assessments before commencing any demolition work, ensure safe work procedures are implemented and provide effective supervision to prevent accidents that could result in serious injury or loss of life," it added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: City council penalises driver caught on video littering at Magazine Road Ghaut
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: The Penang Island City council (MBPP) has issued a compound notice to the owner of a vehicle caught on video allegedly throwing rubbish from a moving car at Magazine Road Ghaut.
On May 29, MBPP said it became aware of a video circulating on Facebook showing the act, which was uploaded by China Press.
The council, in a statement, said it carried out an investigation and subsequently took enforcement action in accordance with existing laws.
It said a compound notice was issued to the vehicle owner for the offence of disposing of rubbish from a vehicle at Magazine Road Ghaut.
The enforcement action was taken based on evidence obtained regarding the act of throwing waste from a private vehicle, which is an offence under the Street, Drainage and Building (Cleanliness and Public Safety) By-Laws 1980.
MBPP said irresponsible behaviour such as littering from vehicles not only pollutes the environment but also poses a risk to road safety and the comfort of other road users.
It reminded the public to comply with public cleanliness regulations and to adopt responsible behaviour in maintaining a clean environment.
The council added that enforcement efforts will continue to be intensified through monitoring, video recordings, public complaints and integrated operations.
"Cooperation from all parties is much appreciated to ensure Penang remains a clean, safe and sustainable city for present and future generations," it said.
Members of the public can channel complaints on illegal dumping to the MBPP hotline at 04-2637637 / 7000 or the Municipal Services Department at 04-2633000 during office hours.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fire damages historic Odeon building in George Town
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: A fire broke out at the former Odeon Cinema building along Penang Road tonight, sending firefighters rushing to contain the blaze at one of the state's most recognisable heritage landmarks.
The building, which housed the Midnight Black nightclub, was engulfed in flames in the 8.30pm incident.
Photographs of the fire have since circulated on
social media.
Tanjung member of parliament Lim Hui Ying said she and Chief Minister Chow Kon Yeow rushed to the scene shortly after being informed of the incident.
"For now, we were told no one is injured in the incident," she said.
Lim thanked personnel from the Lebuh Pantai fire and rescue station as well as volunteer fire brigades for their swift response in bringing the fire under control.
She also expressed appreciation to all agencies involved in rescue, firefighting and coordination efforts at the scene.
The cause of the fire has yet to be determined and investigations are ongoing.
The affected building occupies the site of the historic Odeon Cinema, one of Penang's most storied entertainment landmarks.
Originally known as Theatre Royal before becoming King's Theatre and later Odeon Cinema, the premises had been a fixture of the state's entertainment scene since the early 20th century.
In 1940, the cinema underwent extensive modernisation and reopened as Odeon Cinema, featuring advanced projection equipment, air-conditioning and sound systems for its time.
Throughout the decades, it screened Hollywood productions, classics, Chinese-language films, Hong Kong action blockbusters, as well as Tamil and Hindi movies.
The cinema ceased operations on July 31, 2014, marking the end of one of Malaysia's last standalone single-screen cinemas dedicated primarily to Indian-language films.
Following its closure, the building underwent several transformations, housing various entertainment and lifestyle concepts before becoming Midnight Black, a nightclub that opened in 2022.
Many Penangites regard the building as an important part of the island's cultural and cinematic heritage, having served generations of moviegoers and entertainment seekers for more than eight decades.
